The verses on this meaning are many; among them are those indicating that the ranks of Paradise and the depths of the Fire are according to the levels and ranks of actions.

It is known that action of any kind is among the emanations of knowledge (رشحات العلم); it emanates from a heart-felt belief that suits it. The Exalted has argued for the disbelief of the Jews, the corruption of the polytheists' conscience, the hypocrisy of the hypocrites among the Muslims, and the faith of a number of prophets and believers by their actions and deeds in very many verses whose mention would be lengthy. Thus, action, whatever it may be, accompanies what suits it of knowledge and indicates it.

Conversely, every type of action necessitates what suits it of knowledge, producing it and anchoring it in the soul, as the Exalted said: **وَالَّذِينَ جَاهَدُوا فِينَا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنَا وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لَمَعَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** (As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous) [Al-Ankabut: 69], and the Exalted said: **وَاعْبُدْ رَبَّكَ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَكَ الْيَقِينُ** (And worship your Lord until conviction comes to you) [Al-Hijr: 99], and He also said: **ثُمَّ كَانَ عَاقِبَةَ الَّذِينَ أَسَاءُوا السُّوأَىٰ أَن كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ وَكَانُوا بِهَا يَسْتَهْزِئُونَ** (Then the fate of those who committed misdeeds was that they denied the signs of Allah and they used to deride them) [Ar-Rum: 10], and He said: **فَأَعْقَبَهُمْ نِفَاقًا فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يَلْقَوْنَهُ بِمَا أَخْلَفُوا اللَّهَ مَا وَعَدُوهُ وَبِمَا كَانُوا يَكْذِبُونَ** (So He caused hypocrisy to take root in their hearts until the day they will meet Him, because they went back on what they had promised Allah, and because they used to lie) [At-Tawbah: 77]. The verses on this meaning are also many, all indicating that action—whether righteous or wicked—gives birth to the types of gnosis and ignorance (which are sciences contrary to the truth) that suit it.

The Exalted said—and it is like a comprehensive word regarding righteous action and beneficial knowledge: **إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ** (To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates the righteous deed) [Fatir: 10]. He explained that the function of the "good word" (الكلم الطيب)—which is true belief—is to ascend to Allah the Exalted and bring its possessor close to Him, and the function of the "righteous deed" is to elevate this knowledge and belief. It is known that the elevation of knowledge in its ascent is only by its sincerity from doubt and suspicion, the perfection of the soul's orientation toward it, and the heart not being divided between it and others (which is absolute polytheism). Thus, whenever its sincerity from doubt and hesitations is perfected, its ascent and elevation intensify.

The wording of the verse is not devoid of indication of that, for it expressed the "good word" with "ascent" (الصعود) and described the "action" with "elevation" (الرفع). Ascent is the opposite of descent, just as elevation is the opposite of lowering. These two—ascent and elevation—are descriptions by which the one moving from below to above is characterized in relation to the two sides: he is "ascending" (صاعد) in view of his aiming for the height and his proximity to it, and "elevated" (مرتفع) from the aspect of his separation from the bottom and his distance from it. Thus, action distances the human being and separates him from the world and from clinging to the earth, by turning his soul away from attachment to its distracting ornaments and the scattering and division caused by these vanishing, non-enduring pieces of information. Whenever the elevation and height increase, the ascent of the good word increases, and gnosis is purified from the taints of illusions and the filth of doubts. It is also known, as mentioned, that righteous action has ranks and degrees; thus, for every degree of righteous action, there is an elevation of the good word and a generation of true divine sciences and gnosis according to its state. The discussion regarding wicked action and its lowering of the human being is similar to the discussion regarding righteous action and its elevation, and some discussion on that has already passed in the interpretation of the saying of the Exalted: **اهْدِنَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ** [Al-Hamd: 6].

Thus, it has appeared that people, according to the levels of their proximity and distance from Him, the Exalted, have different levels of action and knowledge. The necessary consequence is that what the people of one level and degree receive is different from what the people of another level and degree receive, whether above or below it. Thus, it has become clear that the Qur’an has different, arranged meanings.

Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned categories of His servants and specified each category with a type of knowledge and gnosis not found in the other category—such as the "sincere ones" (المخلصين), to whom He specified the knowledge of the attributes of their Lord with true knowledge. The Exalted said: **سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبَادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** (Clear is Allah of what they allege, except for Allah’s chosen servants) [As-Saffat: 159-160]. He specified for them other things of gnosis and knowledge whose explanation will come, Allah willing. And like the "possessors of certainty" (الموقنين), to whom He specified the witnessing of the kingdom (ملكوت) of the heavens and the earth. The Exalted said: **وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُرِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ مَلَكوتَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ** (Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certainty) [Al-An'am: 75]. And like the "penitent ones" (المنيبين), to whom He specified remembrance (التذكر). The Exalted said: **وَمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ إِلَّا مَن يُنِيبُ** (and no one takes admonition except him who turns [to Allah] penitently) [Al-Mu'min: 13]. And like the "knowers" (العالمين), to whom He specified the reasoning of the parables of the Qur’an. The Exalted said: **وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثَالُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ وَمَا يَعْقِلُهَا إِلَّا الْعَالِمُونَ** (We draw these parables for mankind; but no one grasps them except the learned) [Al-Ankabut: 43]. And it is as if they are the "possessors of intellect" (اولوا الألباب) and those who reflect (المتدبرون) due to the saying of the Exalted: **أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ أَمْ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبٍ أَقْفَالُهَا** (Do they not contemplate the Qur’an, or are there locks on the hearts?) [Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: 24], and His saying: **أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا** (Do they not contemplate the Qur’an? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would have surely found much discrepancy in it) [An-Nisa: 82]. For the import of the three verses returns to a single meaning, which is the knowledge of the ambiguous of the Qur’an and its referral to its clear verses. And like the "purified ones" (المطهرون), whom Allah specified with the knowledge of the interpretation of the Book. The Exalted said: **إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ فِي كِتَابٍ مَّكْنُونٍ لَّا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ** [Al-Waqi'ah: 77-79]. And like the "friends of Allah" (الاولياء), who are the people of infatuation and love for Allah, and He specified for them that they do not turn their attention to anything except Allah, the Glorified; therefore, they do not fear anything nor do they grieve for anything. The Exalted said: **أَلَا إِنَّ أَوْلِيَاءَ اللَّهِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ** (Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve) [Yunus: 62]. And like the "drawn-near" (المقربين), the "chosen" (المجتبين), the "truthful" (الصديقين), the "righteous" (الصالحين), and the "believers" (المؤمنين); for each of them there are characteristics of knowledge and perception they are specified with, which we will discuss in their appropriate places.

Similar to these good stations are evil stations in opposition to them, which have wicked characteristics in the chapter of knowledge and gnosis, and they have possessors such as the disbelievers (الكافرين), the hypocrites (المنافقين), the transgressors (الفاسقين), the wrongdoers (الظالمين), and others. They have portions of ill-understanding and wicked perception of the signs of Allah and His true gnosis. We have omitted their mention for the sake of brevity, and we will address them during the investigations of this book, Allah willing.

**Tenth:** That the Qur’an has an expansiveness in terms of its application to instances (المصاديق) and the explanation of their state. A verse from it is not restricted to the occasion of its revelation, but rather runs in every instance that unites with the occasion of revelation in its underlying reason (ملاكاً)—like parables which are not restricted to their first instances, but rather extend to what suits them. This meaning is what is called the "running of the Qur’an" (جرى القرآن), and some discussion on it has already passed at the beginning of the book.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi: Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) was asked about the clear (المحكم) and the ambiguous (المتشابه). He said: "The clear is that which is acted upon, and the ambiguous is that which is ambiguous to the one ignorant of it."

**I say:** In it is an allusion that the ambiguous is among that which knowledge of it is possible.

In it also, from him (peace be upon him): "The Qur’an is clear and ambiguous: as for the clear, you believe in it, act upon it, and profess it as religion; as for the ambiguous, you believe in it but do not act upon it. It is the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: **وأما الذين في قلوبهم زيغ فيتبعون ما تشابه منه ابتغاء الفتنة وابتغاء تأويله وما يعلم تأويله إلا الله والراسخون في العلم يقولون آمنا به كل من عند ربنا**. And those firm in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم) are the Progeny of Muhammad."

**I say:** A discussion will come regarding the meaning of his saying (peace be upon him): "And those firm in knowledge are the Progeny of Muhammad."

In it also, from Mus'adah ibn Sadaqah, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the abrogating (الناسخ) and the abrogated (المنسوخ), and the clear (المحكم) and the ambiguous (المتشابه). He said: "The abrogating is the fixed that is acted upon; the abrogated is what used to be acted upon then that which abrogated it came; and the ambiguous is what is ambiguous to the one ignorant of it." He said: And in a narration: "The abrogating is the fixed, the abrogated is what has passed, the clear is what is acted upon, and the ambiguous is what resembles each other."

In *al-Kafi*, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him) in a hadith, he said: "The abrogated ones are among the ambiguous ones."

In *al-Uyun*, from al-Rida (peace be upon him): "Whoever refers the ambiguous of the Qur’an to its clear is guided to a straight path." Then he said: "Indeed, in our reports (أخبارنا) there is ambiguous like the ambiguous of the Qur’an, so refer their ambiguous to their clear, and do not follow their ambiguous lest you go astray."

**I say:** The reports, as you see, are close to each other in the interpretation of the ambiguous, and they support what we mentioned in the previous explanation: that ambiguity accepts removal, and that it is only removed by the clear verse's interpretation of it. As for the abrogated being among the ambiguous, it is so as previously mentioned; the aspect of their ambiguity is what appears from them of the continuation and endurance of the ruling, and the abrogating verse explains it by clarifying that its endurance is cut off. As for what he (peace be upon him) mentioned in the report of *al-Uyun*: "Indeed, in our reports there is ambiguous like the ambiguous of the Qur’an and clear like the clear of the Qur’an," widely transmitted (مستفيضة) narrations have been reported from them (peace be upon them) on this meaning. Reason supports it, for the reports only contain what the noble Qur’an contains, and they only explain what it addressed. You have already known previously: that ambiguity is among the descriptions of the meaning indicated by the word—which is its being such that it accepts application to the intended and to others—not among the descriptions of the word in terms of its indication of the meaning, like strangeness (الغرابة) or obscurity (الاجمال), nor among the descriptions of what is more general than the word and the meaning.

In other words: ambiguity only occurred to that which it occurred to of the verses because their expositions run in the manner of parables (الأمثال) in relation to the true divine gnosis. This very meaning exists in the reports; thus, in them is ambiguous and clear just as in the Qur’an. It has been reported from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم that he said: **إنا معاشر الأنبياء نكلم الناس على قدر عقولهم** — "We, the assembly of prophets, speak to people according to the capacity of their intellects."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from his father (peace be upon them): A man said to the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): "Will you describe our Lord for us so that we may increase in love for Him and knowledge of Him?" He became angry and addressed the people, saying among what he said: "O servant of Allah, adhere to what the Qur’an has directed you to of His description, and what the Messenger has preceded you in of His knowledge, and seek light from the light of its guidance; for it is a blessing and wisdom you have been given, so take what you have been given and be among the thankful. And what Satan has burdened you with of that which is not obligatory upon you in the Book, nor is its command in the Sunnah of the Messenger and the Imams of guidance, then delegate its knowledge to Allah, and do not measure the greatness of Allah. And know, O servant of Allah: that those firm in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم) are those whom Allah has enriched from plunging into the barriers struck before the Unseen (الغيوب), so they adhered to the acknowledgment of the entirety of what they were ignorant of its interpretation (تفسيره) from the veiled Unseen. They said: **امنا به كل من عند ربنا** (We believe in it; all of it is from our Lord). Allah has praised their confession of inability to reach what they did not encompass with knowledge, and He named their abandonment of deep-delving into what He did not burden them with researching as 'firmness' (رسوخاً). So restrict yourself to that, and do not measure the greatness of Allah according to the measure of your intellect, lest you be among the perished."

**I say:** His saying (peace be upon him): "And know, O servant of Allah, that those firm in knowledge..." is apparent in that he (peace be upon him) took the "and" (الواو) in the saying of the Exalted: **والراسخون في العلم يقولون** as a particle of commencement (للاستيناف) rather than a conjunction (العطف), as we inferred from the verse. The consequence of that is that the apparent meaning of the verse does not support those firm in knowledge being knowers of its interpretation (تأويله), not that it supports the impossibility of their knowledge of it. Thus, it does not contradict the existence of another exposition indicating it, as previously explained, and this is the apparent meaning of some reports from the Imams of the People of the Household, as will come. His saying (peace be upon him): "whom Allah has enriched from plunging into the barriers struck before the Unseen," is the predicate of "Indeed" (إن). The speech is apparent in inciting the addressee and encouraging him to adhere to the way of those firm in knowledge by confessing ignorance in what he is ignorant of, so that he may be among them. This is a proof for his (peace be upon him) interpretation of "those firm in knowledge" as anyone who adheres to what he knows and does not exceed it to what he is ignorant of. The intended meaning of the "Unseen veiled by barriers" is the meanings intended by the ambiguous verses which are hidden from common understandings; therefore, he followed it by saying second: "so they adhered to the acknowledgment of the entirety of what they were ignorant of its explanation (تفسيره)," and he did not say "of the entirety of what they were ignorant of its interpretation (تأويله)." So understand.

In *al-Kafi*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "We are those firmly grounded in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم), and we know its interpretation (تأويله)."

**I say:** The narration is not devoid of an apparent indication that the saying of the Exalted, "and those firmly grounded in knowledge" (والراسخون في العلم), is conjoined to the exception in His saying: "No one knows its interpretation except Allah" (وما يعلم تأويله إلا الله). However, this appearance is dispelled by the explanation provided previously and the preceding narration. It is not entirely far-fetched that what is meant by interpretation (التأويل) here is the meaning intended by the ambiguous (المتشابه); for this sense of interpretation—which is synonymous with the explanation of the ambiguous—was common among the people during the first era of Islam.

As for his saying (peace be upon him): "We are those firmly grounded in knowledge," it has already been mentioned in a narration by al-Ayyashi from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he said: "Those firmly grounded in knowledge are the Progeny of Muhammad (آل محمد)." This sentence is also reported in other narrations. All of this falls under the principle of "flow and application" (الجري والانطباق) — *the principle that Quranic verses apply to new instances and individuals over time beyond their original occasion of revelation* — as evidenced by the preceding and forthcoming narrations.

In *al-Kafi* as well, from Hisham ibn al-Hakam, who said: "Abu al-Hasan Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him) said to me..." until he said: "O Hisham, indeed Allah recounted concerning a righteous people that they said: **ربنا لا تزغ قلوبنا بعد إذ هديتنا وهب لنا من لدنك رحمة إنك أنت الوهاب** (Our Lord, do not make our hearts swerve after You have guided us, and bestow Your mercy on us from Yourself. Indeed You are the All-munificent). They realized that hearts swerve and return to their blindness and ruin. Indeed, he who does not intellectually comprehend (يعقل) from Allah does not fear Allah; and he who does not intellectually comprehend from Allah, his heart does not fasten upon a firm gnosis (معرفة ثابتة) that he beholds and whose reality he finds in his heart. No one can be like that unless his speech confirms his action, and his inner secret (سره) accords with his outward conduct (علانيته); because Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه), did not point to the hidden inner dimension (الباطن الخفي) of the intellect except through an outward manifestation (ظاهر) from it and a speaker on its behalf."

**I say:** His saying (peace be upon him), "he who does not intellectually comprehend from Allah does not fear Allah," is in the sense of the saying of the Exalted: **إِنَّمَا يَخْشَى اللَّهَ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ الْعُلَمَاءُ** (Only those of Allah’s servants who possess knowledge fear Him). His saying (peace be upon him), "and he who does not intellectually comprehend from Allah..." etc., is the finest explanation of the meaning of being "firmly grounded in knowledge" (الرسوخ في العلم). This is because as long as a matter is not intellectually grasped with true comprehension, the paths of possibilities regarding it are not closed, and the heart remains agitated in its assent to it. But when the intellectual comprehension is complete and the heart is fastened upon it, it does not oppose it by following the whims (الهوى) that contradict it. Thus, what is in his heart becomes manifest in his limbs, and what he says is what he does. His saying, "No one can be like that..." etc., is an explanation of the sign of being firmly grounded in knowledge.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Tabarani narrated from Anas, Abu Umamah, Wathilah ibn al-Asqa', and Abu al-Darda' that the Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) was asked about those firmly grounded in knowledge, and he said: "He whose oath is pious, whose tongue is truthful, whose heart is upright, and he who guards his stomach and his private parts—such a one is among those firmly grounded in knowledge."

**I say:** It is possible to interpret this narration in a way that returns to the meaning of the previous hadith.

In *al-Kafi*, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "Indeed, those firmly grounded in knowledge are those whose knowledge does not contain contradiction (لا يختلف)."

**I say:** This is applicable to the verse, for "those firmly grounded in knowledge" is contrasted therein with His saying: "those in whose hearts is swerving" (الذين في قلوبهم زيغ). Thus, the firmness of knowledge (رسوخ العلم) consists in the absence of contradiction and doubt in the scholar.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Jarir, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Umm Salamah: "The Messenger of Allah used to frequently say in his supplication: 'O Allah, Turner of hearts, make my heart firm upon Your religion.' I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, do hearts indeed turn?' He said: 'Yes, Allah has not created any human being from the children of Adam except that his heart is between two fingers of the fingers of Allah; if He wills, He keeps it upright, and if He wills, He makes it swerve...'" (the hadith).

**I say:** This meaning has been narrated through numerous chains from several companions, such as Jabir, Nawwas ibn Sam'an, Abdullah ibn Umar, and Abu Hurayrah. The most famous in this regard is what is in the hadith of Nawwas: "The heart of the son of Adam is between two fingers of the fingers of the All-Beneficent (الرحمن)." Al-Sharif al-Radi (as I believe) narrated this phrasing in *al-Majazat al-Nabawiyyah*.

It is narrated from Ali (peace be upon him) that it was said to him: "Do you possess anything of revelation (الوحي)?" He said: "No, by Him who split the grain and created the soul, except that Allah grants a servant understanding (فهماً) of His Book."

**I say:** This is among the most brilliant of hadiths. At the very least, it indicates that the wonders of gnosis (أعاجيب المعارف) issued from his scholarly station—which dazzles the intellects—are derived from the Noble Quran.

In *al-Kafi*, from Imam al-Sadiq, from his father, from his ancestors (peace be upon them), he said: "The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) said: 'O people, you are in an abode of truce (دار هدنة), and you are on a journey, and the travel is swift for you. You have seen the night and the day, the sun and the moon, how they wear out every new thing, bring near every distant thing, and bring about every promised thing. So prepare the equipment for the long passage (المجاز).'" He said: "Then al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad stood up and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, what is the abode of truce?' He replied: 'An abode of reaching and cutting off. So when trials (الفتن) confuse you like pieces of a dark night, then hold fast to the Quran, for it is an intercessor whose intercession is accepted (شافع مشفع), and a credible pleader (ماحل مصدق). Whoever puts it before him, it leads him to Paradise; and whoever puts it behind him, it drives him to the Fire. It is the guide that points to the best path. It is a book containing detail (تفصيل), clarification (بيان), and attainment (تحصيل). It is the Decisive Word (الفصل), not a jest (الهزل). It has an outward (ظهر) and an inward (بطن); its outward is judgment (حكم) and its inward is knowledge (علم). Its outward is elegant (أنيق) and its inward is deep (عميق). It has boundaries (تخوم), and upon its boundaries are boundaries. Its wonders cannot be counted, and its marvels do not wear out. In it are the lamps of guidance (مصابيح الهدى) and the lighthouse of wisdom (منار الحكمة), and a guide to gnosis (المعرفة) for one who recognizes the description. So let the observer cast his gaze and let his vision reach the description, that he may be saved from destruction and be freed from entanglement. For reflection (التفكر) is the life of the heart of the insightful one, just as the seeker of light walks in the darknesses. So you must seek a good exit and minimize hesitation.'"

**I say:** Al-Ayyashi narrated it in his *tafsir* up to his saying: "let the observer cast..."

In *al-Kafi* and the *tafsir* of al-Ayyashi as well, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: "The Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) said: 'The Quran is guidance (هدى) from misguidance, a clarification (تبيان) from blindness, a pardon from stumbling, a light (نور) from darkness, a radiance from events, a protection (عصمة) from destruction, right-guidance (رشد) from error, a clarification from trials, and a reaching from this world to the Hereafter. In it is the perfection of your religion, and no one turns away from the Quran except toward the Fire.'"

**I say:** Narrations in this vein are numerous from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) and the Imams from his Progeny (peace be upon them).

In the *tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from al-Fudayl ibn Yasar, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about this narration: 'There is no verse in the Quran but that it has an outward (ظهر) and an inward (بطن), and there is no letter in it but that it has a limit (حد), and for every limit there is a lookout (مطلع).' What does he mean by 'outward' and 'inward'?" He said: "Its outward is its revelation (تنزيله) and its inward is its interpretation (تأويله). Some of it has passed and some of it has not yet come to be; it flows as the sun and the moon flow. Whenever something of it arrives, it occurs. Allah said: **وَمَا يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا اللَّهُ وَالرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ** (No one knows its interpretation except Allah and those firmly grounded in knowledge); we know it."

**I say:** The narration cited within the narration is what the group [of traditionists] narrated from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) with different wordings, though the meaning is one. As in *Tafsir al-Safi* from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace): "Indeed, the Quran has an outward, an inward, a limit, and a lookout." And in it also from him (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace): "Indeed, the Quran has an outward and an inward, and for its inward is an inward, up to seven inwards."

His saying (peace be upon him), "Some of it has passed and some of it is to come," apparently refers back to the Quran in consideration of its containing both the revelation and the interpretation. Thus, his saying, "it flows as the sun and the moon flow," applies to both together. In the case of revelation, it applies to the "flow" (الجري) which is a technical term in the reports for the application of the speech in its meaning to a referent (المصداق), such as the application of His saying: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَكُونُوا مَعَ الصَّادِقِينَ** (O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and be with the truthful) [9:119], to every group of believers existing in ages subsequent to the time of the verse's revelation. This is one type of application. Another is the application of the verses of Jihad to the struggle against the self (جهاد النفس), and the application of the verses of the hypocrites to the transgressors among the believers; this is another type of application, more subtle than the first. Another is their application to those who practice self-watchfulness (المراقبة), remembrance (الذكر), and presence (الحضور) regarding their shortcomings and negligence in the remembrance of Allah the Exalted; this is another type, more subtle than what preceded it. And another is their application to them regarding their essential deficiency (القصور الذاتي) in fulfilling the right of Lordship (حق الربوبية); this is another type, the most subtle of all.

From here, it becomes clear, firstly: that the Quran has levels of intended meanings according to the levels of its people and their stations. Those who investigate the stations of faith (الإيمان) and sainthood (الولاية) have depicted meanings more subtle than what we have mentioned.

**Secondly:** that the outward (الظهر) and the inward (البطن) are relative matters. Every outward is an inward relative to what is more outward than it, and vice versa, as appears from the following narration.

In the *tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Jabir, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about something regarding the interpretation of the Quran and he answered me. Then I asked him a second time and he answered me with a different answer. I said: 'May I be your ransom! You had answered this question with an answer other than this before today!' He said: 'O Jabir, indeed the Quran has an inward, and for the inward is an inward; and it has an outward, and for the outward is an outward. O Jabir, there is nothing further from the intellects of men than the interpretation of the Quran. Indeed, a verse may have its beginning concerning one thing, its middle concerning another, and its end concerning another, yet it is connected speech that can be directed toward various aspects.'"

In it also from him (peace be upon him) in a hadith, he said: "If a verse, when revealed concerning a people, were to die when those people died, nothing of the Quran would remain. However, the Quran flows from its beginning to its end as long as the heavens and the earth remain. For every people there is a verse they recite, and they are part of it, whether for good or for evil."

In *al-Ma'ani*, from Humran ibn A'yan, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the outward of the Quran and its inward. He said: 'Its outward is those concerning whom the Quran was revealed, and its inward is those who acted according to their deeds; what was revealed concerning the former flows into the latter.'"

In *Tafsir al-Safi*, from Ali (peace be upon him): "There is no verse but that it has four meanings: an outward (ظاهر), an inward (باطن), a limit (حد), and a lookout (مطلع). The outward is the recitation (التلاوة), the inward is the understanding (الفهم), the limit is the rulings of the lawful and the prohibited, and the lookout is what Allah intends from the servant through it."

**I say:** What is meant by "recitation" is the outward indication of the word, evidenced by the fact that he (peace be upon him) counted it among the meanings. Thus, what is meant by "understanding" in his explanation of the inward is the meaning that lies within the outward. What is meant by his saying, "it is the rulings of the lawful and the prohibited," is the outward gnoses received from the Quran in the initial or middle levels, in contrast to the "lookout" which is the highest level. Or, the limit and the lookout are relative, just as the outward and the inward are relative, as you have learned previously. Thus, every higher level is a lookout relative to the lower one.

The word *muttala'* (مطلع) is either with a *damma* on the *mim*, a doubled *ta*, and a *fatha* on the *lam*—a noun of place from "beholding" (الإطلاع)—or with a *fatha* on the *mim* and *lam* and a *sukun* on the *ta*—a noun of place from "rising" (الطلوع). It is what Allah intends from the servant through the verse, as he (peace be upon him) mentioned.

These four matters have been mentioned in the well-known prophetic report as follows: "The Quran was revealed according to seven modes (أحرف); for every verse of it there is an outward and an inward, and for every limit there is a lookout." In one narration: "and for every limit and lookout."

The meaning of his saying (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace), "and for every limit there is a lookout," according to one of the narrations, is that for each of the outward and the inward—which is a limit—there is a lookout that overlooks it. This is the apparent meaning. It is possible that what is in the other narration, "and for every limit and lookout," returns to this, such that the meaning would be: for each of them there is a limit which is itself, and a lookout which is that which the limit reaches, thereby overlooking the interpretation (التأويل). However, this does not apparently harmonize with what is in the narration of Ali (peace be upon him): "There is no verse but that it has four meanings..." etc., unless it is meant that it has four considerations of meaning, even if some of them might coincide with others.

Accordingly, what is gathered from the meanings of these four matters is: the outward (الظهر) is the apparent meaning that first emerges from the verse; the inward (البطن) is that which is beneath the outward, whether it be one or many, near to it or far, with or without a medium; the limit (الحد) is the meaning itself, whether it be outward or inward; and the lookout (المطلع) is the meaning from which the limit emerged, which is its inward connected to it. So understand.

In the hadith narrated through the chains of both groups [Sunni and Shi'a] from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace): "The Quran was revealed according to seven modes (أحرف)."

**I say:** Although the hadith is narrated with variations in its wording, its meaning is narrated extensively (مستفيضاً), and the narrations are close in meaning, reported by both the general [Sunni] and the specific [Shi'a] scholars. There has been intense disagreement regarding the meaning of the hadith, sometimes reaching forty opinions. What eases the matter is that within the reports themselves is an explanation of these seven modes, and upon that is the reliance.

In some reports: "The Quran was revealed according to seven modes: command (أمر), prohibition (زجر), encouragement (ترغيب), warning (ترهيب), polemic (جدل), stories (قصص), and parables (مثل)." In others: "prohibition, command, lawful, prohibited, decisive (محكم), ambiguous (متشابه), and parables."

From Ali (peace be upon him): "Indeed, Allah revealed the Quran according to seven categories, each of which is sufficient and healing: command, prohibition, encouragement, warning, polemic, parables, and stories."

Thus, it is necessary to interpret the "seven modes" as the categories of discourse and types of clarification. They are seven despite their unity in the call to Allah and His straight path (الصراط المستقيم). It can be inferred from this narration that the foundations of divine gnoses (أصول المعارف الإلهية) are restricted to parables (الأمثال), for the rest of the seven do not suit them except through a kind of figurative extension, as is not hidden.

## (Another Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Safi*, from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace): "Whoever interprets the Quran according to his own opinion (رأيه), let him take his seat in the Fire."

**I say:** This meaning has been narrated by both groups, and there are other hadiths with this meaning narrated from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) and the Imams of the People of the House (peace be upon them).

In *Munyat al-Murid*, from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace), he said: "Whoever speaks concerning the Quran without knowledge (بغير علم), let him take his seat in the Fire."

**I say:** Abu Dawud narrated it in his *Sunan*.

In it from him (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace), he said: "Whoever speaks concerning the Quran without knowledge will come on the Day of Resurrection bridled with a bridle of fire."

In it from him (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace), he said: "Whoever speaks concerning the Quran according to his own opinion and hits the mark, he has still erred."

**I say:** Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, and al-Nasa'i narrated it.

In it from him (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace), he said: "What I fear most for my community after me is a man who takes up the Quran and places it in other than its proper places."

In the *tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Abu Basir, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: "Whoever interprets the Quran according to his own opinion, if he hits the mark he is not rewarded, and if he errs, he is further [from the truth] than the heaven."

In it from Ya'qub ibn Yazid, from Yasir, from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him), he said: "Opinion (الرأي) concerning the Book of Allah is disbelief (كفر)."

**I say:** There are other narrations with this meaning reported in *al-Uyun*, *al-Khisal*, the *tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, and others.

His saying (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace), "Whoever interprets the Quran according to his own opinion"—opinion (الرأي) is a belief resulting from personal effort (اجتهاد), and it is sometimes applied to speaking out of whim (الهوى) and personal preference (الاستحسان). In any case, when the word "opinion" was mentioned in construct with the pronoun ("his opinion"), it became known that what is meant is not the prohibition of absolute *ijtihad* in interpreting the Quran—such that it would by implication be a command to follow and restrict oneself to what has been reported in the narrations concerning the interpretation of verses from the Prophet and his Progeny (may Allah bless him and them), as the People of Hadith (أهل الحديث) hold. Moreover, this would contradict the many verses indicating that the Quran is a clear Arabic [book] and those commanding reflection (التدبر) upon it, as well as the many narrations commanding referral to the Quran and the weighing of reports against it.

Rather, the construct in his saying "according to his own opinion" conveys the meaning of exclusivity, singularity, and independence—that the interpreter acts independently in interpreting the Quran using the means he possesses for understanding Arabic speech, thus measuring the speech of the Exalted by the speech of humans. For if a piece of speech from any speaker reaches us, we do not hesitate to apply the rules used in uncovering the communicative intent and judge thereby that he intended such-and-forth, just as we proceed in confessions, testimonies, and other matters. All of this is because our clarification is based on what we know of the language and what we are accustomed to regarding the referents of words, whether literal or metaphorical.

The Quranic clarification (البيان القرآني) does not proceed in this manner, as explained in previous discussions. Rather, it is speech connected to one another while being distinct; some of it speaks through others and some of it bears witness to others, as Ali (peace be upon him) said. Therefore, what is obtained from a single verse by applying the rules established in the related sciences is not sufficient for uncovering the intended meaning unless one considers all the verses relevant to it and strives in reflecting upon them, as appears from the saying of the Exalted: **أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا** (Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would surely have found much discrepancy in it) [4:82]. This was already explained in the discussion on conciseness (الإيجاز) and other topics.

Thus, the "interpretation by opinion" (التفسير بالرأي) that is prohibited is a matter pertaining to the method of uncovering (طريق الكشف) rather than the thing uncovered (المكشوف). In other words, he (peace be upon him) only prohibited understanding His speech in the same way that the speech of others is understood, even if this way of understanding might sometimes coincide with reality. The proof for this is his saying (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) in the other narration: "Whoever speaks concerning the Quran according to his own opinion and hits the mark, he has still erred." For the judgment of error despite hitting the mark is only because the error lies in the method. Likewise is his saying (peace be upon him) in the hadith of al-Ayyashi: "if he hits the mark he is not rewarded."

This is supported by the state of affairs during the time of the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace), for the Quran had not yet been compiled; there were only chapters or scattered verses in the hands of the people. Thus, in interpreting every single piece of it, there was a danger of falling into what was contrary to the intended meaning.

The upshot is: that which is prohibited is independence in interpreting the Quran and the interpreter's reliance on himself without referring to another. Its implication is the necessity of seeking aid from another by referring to it. This "other" is inevitably either the Book or the Sunnah. That it be the Sunnah contradicts the Quran and the Sunnah itself, which commands referral to the Quran and the weighing of reports against it. Thus, nothing remains for referral and seeking aid in interpreting the Quran except the Quran itself.

From here, the status of how they interpreted the hadith of "interpretation by opinion" becomes clear, for they have diverged in its meaning into several opinions:

**First:** That what is meant is interpretation without possessing the sciences that permit interpretation, which are fifteen sciences according to what al-Suyuti listed in *al-Itqan*: lexicography, grammar, morphology, etymology, rhetoric (meanings, eloquence, and embellishment), orthoepy, foundations of religion, foundations of jurisprudence, occasions of revelation as well as stories, the abrogating (الناسخ) and the abrogated (المنسوخ), jurisprudence, hadiths explaining the summarized and ambiguous matters, and the "science of gift" (علم الموهبة)—by the latter he means what the prophetic hadith pointed to: "Whoever acts upon what he knows, Allah bequeaths to him the knowledge of what he did not know."

**Second:** That what is meant is the interpretation of the ambiguous (المتشابه) which no one knows except Allah.

**Third:** Interpretation that confirms a corrupt school of thought, by making the school of thought the foundation and the interpretation a follower, so it is referred back to it by any possible means, even if weak.

**Fourth:** Interpreting that the intent of Allah the Exalted is such-and-forth with certainty without evidence.

**Fifth:** Interpretation based on personal preference (الاستحسان) and whim (الهوى). These five aspects were cited by Ibn al-Naqib as mentioned by al-Suyuti in *al-Itqan*. Here are other aspects we shall follow them with:

**Sixth:** That what is meant is speaking regarding the difficult parts of the Quran with that which is not known from the schools of the predecessors among the companions and the followers; in this is exposure to the wrath of Allah the Exalted.

**Seventh:** Speaking regarding the Quran with that which one knows the truth to be otherwise; these two were cited by Ibn al-Anbari.

**Eighth:** That what is meant is speaking regarding the Quran without knowledge and verification, whether one knows the truth to be otherwise or not.

**Ninth:** Taking the outward (ظاهر) of the Quran based on the premise that it has no apparent meaning (ظهور), but rather one follows in the case of the verse the text (النص) reported from the Infallible (المعصوم). This would not be an interpretation of the verse but a following of the text, and interpretation would thus be among the affairs restricted to the Infallible.

**Tenth:** Taking the outward of the Quran based on the premise that it has an apparent meaning which we do not understand, but rather what is followed in interpreting the verse is the text from the Infallible.

These are ten aspects, and it might be possible to refer some of them back to others. In any case, they are aspects devoid of evidence; moreover, some are clearly false or their falsehood becomes apparent from what preceded in previous discussions, so we shall not prolong with repetition.

In short, what is gathered from the narrations and the verses that support them—such as His saying: "Do they not contemplate the Quran..." the verse; and His saying: **الَّذِينَ جَعَلُوا الْقُرْآنَ عِضِينَ** (those who have reduced the Quran to fragments) [15:91]; and His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِي آيَاتِنَا لَا يَخْفَوْنَ عَلَيْنَا أَفَمَن يُلْقَىٰ فِي النَّارِ خَيْرٌ أَم مَّن يَأْتِي آمِنًا يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ** (Indeed those who pervert Our signs are not hidden from Us. Is he who is thrown into the Fire better, or he who comes [before Us] in safety on the Day of Resurrection?) [41:40]; and His saying: **يُحَرِّفُونَ الْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِ** (pervert the words from their contexts) [4:46]; and His saying: **وَلَا تَقْفُ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ** (Do not follow that of which you have no knowledge) [17:36], among others—is that the prohibition in the narrations is directed toward the method (الطريق). It is that one should traverse in interpreting His speech the path traversed in interpreting the speech of others among the created beings.

The difference between His speech and the speech of others is not in the manner of using words, constructing sentences, or employing linguistic arts; for it is Arabic speech in which all that is observed in Arabic speech has been observed. He the Exalted said: **وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسَانِ قَوْمِهِ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ** (We did not send any apostle except with the language of his people, so that he might make [Our messages] clear to them) [14:4]; and He said: **وَهَٰذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُّبِينٌ** (and this is a clear Arabic language) [16:103]; and He said: **إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ** (We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason) [43:3].

Rather, the difference is from the perspective of the intended meaning and the referent (المصداق) to which the concept of the speech applies.

To clarify: because our existence is attached to physical nature and our temporary dwelling is in the material world, we have become accustomed to the material referent for every meaning. We have become habituated to bodies and physical things. Thus, when we hear the speech of one of the people who are like us recounting the state of some matter, and we understand its meaning from him, we carry it toward what is familiar to us of the referent and the system governing it, knowing that he only intends that because he is like us and perceives only that. At that point, the system governing the referent returns to govern the concept; thus, the general might be specified by it, or the specific generalized, or the concept modified by any other modification—which is what we call the modification by rational, non-linguistic context (القرائن العقلية).

For example, if we hear one of our dear ones, possessed of sovereignty and wealth, saying: "There is nothing but that we have its treasuries," and we grasp the concept of the speech and the meanings of its individual words, we judge in the stage of application to the referent that he has fortified, limited buildings that contain many objects; for this is how a treasury (الخزانة) is made if it is made. And we judge that he has therein an abundant amount of gold, silver, paper, furniture, adornment, and weapons; for these are the things that can be stored and preserved. As for the earth, the heaven, the land, the sea, the stars, and the human being—though they are "things"—they are not stored or accumulated. Therefore, we judge that what is meant by "thing" is some of its non-exhaustive individuals, and likewise by "treasuries" a few out of many. Thus, the system existing in the referent—which is that many things are not stored, and that what is stored is only stored in a fortified building safe from theft and raid—has necessitated a strange restriction in the absolute sense of the concept of "thing" and "treasuries."

Then, if we hear Allah the Exalted revealing to His Messenger His saying: **وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ** (There is not a thing but that its treasuries are with Us) [15:21], if our minds do not rise above their simple, primary level, we would interpret His speech exactly as we interpreted the speech of a single human being, even though we have absolutely no evidence for that. This would be an interpretation based on what we see, without knowledge.

If our minds rise slightly above that, and we acknowledge that He the Exalted does not store wealth—especially when we hear Him saying at the end of the verse: **وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ** (and We do not send it down except in a known measure), and He also says: **وَمَا أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مِن رِّزْقٍ فَأَحْيَا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا** (and the provision that Allah sends down from the sky, with which He revives the earth after its death) [45:5]—we would judge that what is meant by "thing" is provision (الرزق) consisting of bread and water, and that what is meant by its "sending down" is the falling of rain, because we do not perceive anything descending from the sky other than rain. Thus, the storing of everything with Allah and then its descent in measure is a metaphor for the storing of rain and its descent to prepare food materials. This is also an interpretation based on what we see, without knowledge, for it has no basis except that we do not know of anything descending from the sky other than rain; and what we have here is a lack of knowledge, not knowledge of the lack.

If we transcend this level as well and avoid speaking regarding the Quran without knowledge, and we keep the speech in its complete absolute sense, judging that His saying: "There is not a thing but that its treasuries are with Us" explains the matter of creation—except that since we do not doubt that the things we find renewed by creation, such as humans, animals, plants, and others, do not descend from the sky but rather occur as events on earth—we would judge that His saying: "There is not a thing but that its treasuries are with Us" is a metaphor for the compliance of things in their existence to the will of Allah, and that the will is like a storehouse in which all created things are stored, and only that which His will attaches to emerges from it and descends from Him. This also, as you see, is an interpretation of the verse based on what we see, without knowledge, for we have no basis in it except that we find things not descending from Allah in the sense we are familiar with regarding descent, and we have no knowledge of any other sense.

If you reflect upon what Allah the Exalted has described in His Book of the names of His essence, His attributes, His acts, His angels, His books, His messengers, the Resurrection and what pertains to it, the rulings of His laws and their underlying reasons (ملاكاتها), and you reflect upon what we aim for in their interpretation by employing rational contexts, you will find that all of that is of the nature of interpretation by opinion without knowledge, and a perversion of the words from their contexts.

It was already mentioned in the fifth chapter of the discussion on the decisive and the ambiguous that the Quranic clarifications regarding divine gnoses are like parables, or they are parables relative to their symbolized realities. They have been dispersed in various verses and explained with different clarifications so that what might be hidden in meaning in some verses may be clarified by others. Therefore, some of them bear witness to others, and one verse interprets another. Were it not for that, the matter of divine gnoses in their realities would be disrupted, and it would not be possible to escape from speaking without knowledge in interpreting the verse, as previously explained.

From here it becomes clear: that interpretation by opinion, as we have explained it, is not devoid of speaking without knowledge, as the previous prophetic hadith points out: "Whoever speaks concerning the Quran without knowledge, let him take his seat in the Fire."

From here it also becomes clear: that this leads to the appearance of contradiction between the Quranic verses, insofar as it invalidates the conceptual order (الترتيب المعنوي) existing in their contents. This leads to placing the verse in other than its proper place and putting the word in other than its context. It necessitates the interpretation (تأويل) of some of the Quran or most of its verses by turning them away from their outward meaning, just as the Compulsionists (المجبرة) interpret the verses of choice, and the Delegationists (المفوضة) interpret the verses of predestination. Most schools of thought in Islam are not devoid of interpreting Quranic verses whose outward meaning does not agree with their school; they cling in that to the tail of interpretation based on rational context, which is their saying: "Such-and-forth outward meaning has been proven contrary to reason, so the speech must be turned away from it."

In short, this leads to the mixing of verses with one another through the invalidation of their order, and the repelling of the purposes of some by others. Both intended meanings are thereby invalidated, for there is no discrepancy in the Quran. The appearance of discrepancy between verses—one with another—is only due to the disruption of the matter and the mixing of the intended meaning in both.

This is what has been expressed in the narrations as "striking some of the Quran against others" (ضرب بعض القرآن ببعض), as in the following narrations:

In *al-Kafi* and the *tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq, from his father (peace be upon them), he said: "No man has ever struck some of the Quran against others except that he disbelieved."

In *al-Ma'ani* and *al-Mahasin* with a chain, and in the *tafsir* of al-Ayyashi from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "No man has ever struck some of the Quran against others except that he disbelieved."

Al-Saduq said: "I asked Ibn al-Walid about the meaning of this hadith, and he said: 'It is that you answer a man in the interpretation of a verse with the interpretation of another verse.'"

**I say:** What he answered with is not devoid of ambiguity. If he meant by it the aforementioned mixing and what is common among researchers in their debates of opposing one verse with another and interpreting some by clinging to others, then it is correct. But if he meant by it interpreting a verse by another verse and seeking evidence from some for others, then it is wrong, and the following two narrations refute it.

In the *tafsir* of al-Nu'mani, with his chain to Isma'il ibn Jabir, who said: "I heard Abu Abdillah Ja'far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq (peace be upon them) saying: 'Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, sent Muhammad and sealed the prophets with him, so there is no prophet after him. And He sent down to him a Book and sealed the books with it, so there is no book after him. He made lawful in it the lawful and prohibited the prohibited; so its lawful is lawful until the Day of Resurrection, and its prohibited is prohibited until the Day of Resurrection. In it is your law and the news of those before you and after you. The Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny) made it an abiding sign (علماً) among his successors (أوصيائه), but the people left them, though they are the witnesses over the people of every time. They turned away from them and then killed them, and followed others and then became sincere in obedience to them until they showed enmity to those who manifested the guardianship (ولاية) of the masters of the affair and sought their sciences. Allah the Glorified said: **فَنَسُوا حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ وَلَا تَزَالُ تَطَّلِعُ عَلَىٰ خَائِنَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ** (they have forgotten a part of what they were reminded of. You will not cease to learn of some treachery on their part) [5:13]. That is because they struck some of the Quran against others; they argued with the abrogated (المنسوخ) while thinking it was the abrogating (الناسخ), they argued with the ambiguous (المتشابه) while seeing it as the decisive (المحكم), they argued with the specific (الخاص) while estimating it to be the general (العام), and they argued with the beginning of the verse and left the reason for its interpretation. They did not look at what opens the speech and what concludes it, and they did not know its entries and exits since they did not take it from its people, so they went astray and led others astray.

And know, may Allah have mercy on you: that whoever does not know from the Book of Allah, Mighty and Majestic, the abrogating from the abrogated, the specific from the general, the decisive from the ambiguous, the permissions (الرخص) from the obligations (العزائم), the Meccan and the Medinan, the occasions of revelation, the vague in the Quran in its disconnected and composed words, what is in it of the science of predestination and decree, the advancement and the delay, the manifest and the deep, the outward and the inward, the beginning and the end, the question and the answer, the disconnection and the connection, the excepted and that which is included, the attribute of what precedes that points to what follows, the emphasized and the detailed, its obligations and permissions, the places of its duties and rulings, and the meaning of its lawful and prohibited in which the heretics perished, the connected words, and that which is carried over to what is before it and what is after it—such a one is not a scholar of the Quran nor is he of its people.

And whenever a claimant claims knowledge of these categories without evidence, he is a doubting liar, fabricating lies against Allah and His Messenger, and his abode is Hell, and an evil destination it is.'"

In *Nahj al-Balaghah* and *al-Ihtijaj*, he (peace be upon him) said: "A case regarding one of the rulings is brought before one of them, and he judges in it according to his own opinion. Then that very same case is brought before another, and he judges in it contrary to the first one's saying. Then the judges gather with that before the Imam who appointed them, and he deems all their opinions correct—while their God is one, their Prophet is one, and their Book is one! Did Allah the Glorified command them to differ and they obeyed Him? Or did He forbid them from it and they disobeyed Him? Or did Allah send down a deficient religion and seek their help to complete it? Or were they partners, having the right to speak and He the duty to be pleased? Or did Allah send down a complete religion but the Messenger (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) fell short in conveying and delivering it? While Allah the Glorified says: **مَّا فَرَّطْنَا فِي الْكِتَابِ مِن شَيْءٍ** (We have not omitted anything from the Book) [6:38], and in it is the clarification of everything. And He mentioned that the Book confirms some of it with others, and that there is no discrepancy in it, so the Glorified said: **وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا** (Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would surely have found much discrepancy in it) [4:82]. Indeed, the Quran's outward is elegant and its inward is deep; its wonders cannot be counted, its marvels do not end, and the darknesses are not dispelled except by it."

**I say:** The narration, as you see, explicitly states that every religious view must end at the Quran. His saying, "in it is the clarification," is a transmission of the verse by meaning.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Sa'd, Ibn al-Durays in his *Fada'il*, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Amr ibn Shu'ayb, from his father, from his grandfather: "The Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) came out to a people who were arguing over the Quran while he was angry. He said: 'By this the nations before you went astray, by their differing with their prophets and striking some of the Book against others.' He said: 'Indeed, the Quran was not revealed for some of it to belie others, but it was revealed for some of it to confirm others. So what you recognize, act upon it; and what is ambiguous to you, believe in it.'"

In it also, Ahmad narrated from another way from Amr ibn Shu'ayb, from his father, from his grandfather, that he heard the Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) [addressing] a people who were disputing. He said: "Those before you only perished by this: they struck some of the Book of Allah against others. Indeed, the Book of Allah was only revealed for some of it to confirm others, so do not belie some of it with others. What you know of it, speak; and what you are ignorant of, entrust it to its scholar."

**I say:** The narrations, as you see, consider "striking some of the Quran against others" as the opposite of "some of the Quran confirming others." It is the mixing between verses regarding the levels of their meanings, and the disruption of the order of their purposes, such as taking the decisive as ambiguous and the ambiguous as decisive, and the like.

Thus, speaking in the Quran by opinion, speaking in the Quran without knowledge—as is the subject of the narrations cited earlier—and striking some of the Quran against others—as is the content of the narrations cited just now—all hover around a single meaning: seeking aid in interpreting the Quran from other than it.

**If you say:** There is no doubt that the Quran was only revealed for people to intellectually grasp and understand it, as He the Exalted said: **إِنَّا أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ لِلنَّاسِ** (Indeed We have sent down the Book to you for the people) [39:41], and He said: **هَٰذَا بَيَانٌ لِّلنَّاسِ** (This is an explanation for mankind) [3:138], among other verses. And there is no doubt that its clarifier is the Messenger (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace), as He the Exalted said: **وَأَنزَلْنَا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ مَا نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ** (We have sent down the Reminder to you so that you may clarify for the people what has been sent down to them) [16:44]. He clarified it for the companions, then the followers took it from them. So what they narrated from him (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) to us is a prophetic clarification that it is not permissible to turn away from or ignore by the text of the Quran. As for what they spoke regarding it without attributing it to the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace), though it does not proceed like the prophetic reports in its authority (حجيتها), the heart is more at rest with it; for what they mentioned in interpreting the verses is either heard from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) or something to which they were guided by the taste acquired from his clarification and teaching (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace). Likewise is what their students among the followers and those who succeeded them mentioned. How could the meanings of the Quran be hidden from them despite their deep roots in Arabic, their effort in receiving it from the source of the Message, and their extreme striving in the jurisprudence of the religion, as history recounts of the efforts of the men of religion in the early era of Islam?

From here it appears: that turning away from their path and tradition, exiting from their consensus, and interpreting a verse with what is not found among their sayings and opinions is an innovation (بدعة); and silence regarding what they were silent about is obligatory.

In what has been narrated from them is sufficiency for whoever wants to understand the Book of Allah the Exalted, for it reaches nearly thousands of narrations. Al-Suyuti mentioned that he brought it to seventeen thousand narrations from the Prophet, the companions, and the followers.

**I say:** It has already been mentioned that the verses which call the people in general—whether disbeliever or believer, those who witnessed the era of revelation or were absent from it—to intellectually grasp the Quran, contemplate it, and reflect upon it, especially His saying: **أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا** (Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would surely have found much discrepancy in it) [4:82], clearly indicate that Quranic gnoses can be attained by the researcher through reflection and investigation, and that what appears as discrepancy between verses is removed thereby. The verse is in the station of challenge (التحدي), and there is no meaning in referring the understanding of the meanings of the verses—in this station—to the understanding of the companions and their students among the followers, or even to the clarification of the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace). For what he clarified is either a meaning that agrees with the outward of the speech—and thus it is among what the word leads to, even if after reflection, contemplation, and investigation—or it is a meaning that does not agree with the outward nor does the speech lead to it—and thus it is among what does not suit the challenge nor is the proof completed by it, and this is obvious.

Yes, the details of the rulings are among what there is no way to receive except from the clarification of the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace), as the Quran referred them to him in His saying: **وَمَا آتَاكُمُ الرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَمَا نَهَاكُمْ عَنْهُ فَانتَهُوا** (Take whatever the Apostle gives you, and relinquish whatever he forbids you) [59:7], and verses with similar meaning; likewise the details of the stories and the Resurrection, for example.

From here it appears that the role of the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) in this station is teaching (التعليم) only. Teaching is merely the guidance of the expert teacher to the mind of the student and his directing him to what is difficult for him to know and attain, not what is impossible to understand without teaching. For teaching is only a facilitation of the path and a bringing near of the destination, not an invention of the path or a creation of the destination. The teacher in his teaching only aims to arrange the scholarly subjects and order them in a way that the student's mind finds easy and familiar, so he does not fall into the toil of arrangement and the labor of organization, thereby wasting life and the gift of strength, or bordering on error in knowledge.

This is what is indicated by the likes of His saying: **وَأَنزَلْنَا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ مَا نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ** (We have sent down the Reminder to you so that you may clarify for the people what has been sent down to them) [16:44], and His saying: **وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ** (and teaches them the Book and wisdom) [62:2]. Thus, the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) only teaches the people and clarifies for them what the Quran itself points to, and what Allah the Glorified clarifies with His speech, and what the people can eventually attain. For if he (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) were clarifying for them meanings for which there is no path to understanding from the speech of Allah the Exalted, that would absolutely not apply to the likes of His saying: **كِتَابٌ فُصِّلَتْ آيَاتُهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ** (a Book whose signs have been elaborated, an Arabic Quran, for a people who have knowledge) [41:3], and His saying: **وَهَٰذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُّبِينٌ** (and this is a clear Arabic language) [16:103].

Moreover, the mutawatir reports from him (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) containing his testament to hold fast to the Quran, to take from it, and to weigh the narrations reported from him (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) against the Book of Allah—their meaning would not be upright unless all that was reported from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) is among what can be derived from the Book. If that were dependent on the clarification of the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace), it would be a fallacious circularity (الدور الباطل), and this is obvious.

Furthermore, what has been reported of the speech of the companions—regardless of its chains—is not devoid of discrepancy among the companions themselves, rather even discrepancy in what is reported from a single one of them, as is not hidden from the researcher who reflects upon their reports. The saying that it is obligatory then to choose one of the different reported opinions from them regarding the verse, and to avoid breaking their consensus and exiting from their group, is rejected by the fact that they themselves did not traverse this path, nor did they commit to this method, nor did they care about the difference among themselves. So how can it be obligatory for others to stand upon what they said, while they were not exclusively granted authority for their speech over others, nor was differing prohibited for others but not for them?

Moreover, this path—which is restricting oneself to what has been reported from the interpreters of the early era of Islam among the companions and the followers regarding the meanings of the Quranic verses—necessitates the cessation of knowledge in its course and the invalidation of investigation in its wake, as is witnessed in what is in our hands of the words of the predecessors and the books authored in *tafsir* in the first centuries of Islam. Nothing has been reported from them in *tafsir* except simple, naive meanings devoid of depth of investigation and precision of sight. So where are the subtleties of gnoses in the Quran to which His saying points: **وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ تِبْيَانًا لِّكُلِّ شَيْءٍ** (and We have sent down the Book to you as a clarification of all things) [16:89]?

As for the improbability that the meanings of the Quran would be hidden from them despite their understanding, earnestness, and striving—it is invalidated by the very discrepancy occurring among them in the meanings of many verses and the contradiction occurring in the words reported from them; for no discrepancy or contradiction can be imagined except with the premise of the truth being hidden and its path being mixed with others.

So the truth is that the path to understanding the Noble Quran is not blocked, and that the divine clarification and the Wise Reminder is itself the path guiding to itself. That is, it does not need a path to clarify its purposes. How can it be imagined that the Book which Allah defined as guidance (هدى), as light (نور), and as a clarification of all things (تبياناً لكل شيء) would be in need of a guide other than itself, seeking light from a light other than itself, and being clarified by a matter other than itself?

**If you say:** It is authentically reported from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) that he said in the last sermon he delivered: "Indeed, I am leaving among you the two weighty things (الثقلين): the greater weighty thing and the lesser weighty thing. As for the greater, it is the Book of my Lord; as for the lesser, it is my Progeny, the People of my House (عترتي أهل بيتي). So observe me in how you treat them, for you shall never go astray as long as you hold fast to them both." Both groups [Sunni and Shi'a] narrated it through mutawatir chains from a large number of the companions of the Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) from him; the scholars of hadith have brought their number to thirty-five companions. In some chains: "They shall never separate until they return to me at the Pond." The hadith indicates the authority of the saying of the People of the House (peace be upon them) regarding the Quran and the obligation of following what has been reported from them in its interpretation and restricting oneself to that; otherwise, it would necessitate separation between them and it.

**I say:** What we mentioned regarding the meaning of following the clarification of the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) just now applies here exactly. The hadith is not intended to invalidate the authority of the outward of the Quran and restrict authority to the outward of the clarification of the People of the House (peace be upon them). How could it be so when he (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) says: "They shall never separate," thus making authority for both together? For the Quran belongs the indication of its meanings and the uncovering of divine gnoses, and for the People of the House belongs the indication of the path and the guidance of the people to its aims and purposes.

Moreover, the like of what was reported from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Progeny and grant them peace) in calling the people to take from the Quran, reflect upon it, and weigh what was reported from him against it, is reported from the People of the House (peace be upon them).

Furthermore, a large number of the interpretative narrations reported from them (peace be upon them) contain the deduction of one verse from another and the seeking of evidence for one meaning from another. This would not be upright unless the meaning were among what the addressee can attain and his mind can independently grasp by its being presented through its designated path.

In addition, there are narrations from them (peace be upon them) that indicate this directly, as narrated in *al-Mahasin* with its chain from Abu Labid al-Bahrani from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) in a hadith, he said: "Whoever claims that the Book of Allah is ambiguous (مبهم) has perished and led others to perish." Close to it is what is in it and in *al-Ihtijaj* from him (peace be upon him), he said: "When I narrate something to you, ask me about it from the Book of Allah..." (the hadith).

Through the preceding commentary (البيان), one can reconcile the various traditions (الأحاديث) indicating the possibility of attaining Quranic gnosis (المعارف القرآنية) and the fact that it is not veiled from the intellects (العقول), with those traditions whose apparent meaning suggests the opposite. An example of the latter is found in *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Jabir, who said: "Abu ‘Abdillah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'Indeed, the Quran has an inner depth (بطن), and that inner depth has an outer surface (ظهر).' Then he said: 'O Jabir, there is nothing further from the intellects of men than it [the Quran]. Indeed, the beginning of a verse (الآية) may be revealed concerning one thing, its middle concerning another, and its end concerning yet another; it is a continuous speech (كلام متصل) that can be interpreted in multiple ways (ينصرف على وجوه).'" This meaning is reported in several narrations. The specific phrase—"there is nothing further... etc."—has also been narrated in some reports from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and it is narrated from ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) that: "The Quran is multifaceted (حمال ذو وجوه)..." to the end of the tradition.

Thus, the method of exegesis (التفسير) that has been encouraged is that which proceeds through its own proper path, while the method that has been forbidden is that which proceeds through any other path. It has become clear that the necessary approach in exegesis is to seek aid from the Quran itself to understand it, and to explain one verse by means of another verse (تفسير الآية بالآية). This is achieved by training oneself through the transmitted reports (الآثار) from the Prophet and his Household (صلى الله عليه وعليهم), thereby cultivating an acquired intuitive taste (ذوق مكتسب) derived from them, before approaching the text. And Allah is the Guide (الهادي).
